content.assessment: Grade-splitting targets economies of scale specifically where they are most plausible — at higher grades requiring specialist teachers — while retaining lower-grade provision where per-pupil overhead is least likely to be offset by consolidation given contested savings evidence. This partially addresses the incentive problem of national minimum class-size thresholds (14-27 pupils), which are more feasible to meet at a single upper-grade site, but the proposal does not resolve the broader contested finding that consolidation savings are not reliably realized once transport and adjustment costs are included.
opportunities: Partial consolidation may be more fiscally sustainable than full closure given Hungary's constrained per-pupil spending, since it captures scale economies only where evidence suggests they are strongest (upper grades/specialist provision) (F-live-education_finance-07).
strengths: Concentrates specialist-teacher resources at the consolidated site, addressing the fiscal inefficiency of thinly spreading scarce specialists across many small schools.

Aligns with national funding formula thresholds only at the upper-grade site, reducing the incentive mismatch between class-size requirements and small-school enrollment realities (implicit via national formula logic underlying F-live-education_finance-07's funding-constraint context).
threats: If site-selection dynamics mirror documented inter-municipal competition, fiscal benefits could be captured unevenly, with some municipalities bearing disproportionate transport or capital costs.

Segregation dynamics via selective church-sector absorption of upper-grade pupils could impose downstream compensatory costs (e.g., targeted equity funding) not currently budgeted, echoing the distributional risks captured in F-live-education_finance-06 even though this finding centers on closure rather than grade-splitting specifically.
weaknesses: International evidence on consolidation savings is contested — rural New York shows net savings of 31.5-61.7%, but multi-state U.S. research and a Texas study found no significant post-consolidation savings, so the fiscal magnitude of benefit for upper-grade consolidation specifically is uncertain (F-live-education_finance-02).

Consolidating upper grades still generates transportation cost offsets for older pupils that are systematically undercounted in closure/consolidation proposals, reducing net fiscal savings relative to headline projections (F-live-education_finance-03).
